__data__.text:
Consumer Protection Act, 2019

--------------------------------------------------

Section 2 - Definitions

Defines key terms such as:
- Consumer
- Service
- Deficiency
- Complaint

Explanation:
This section clarifies who can file complaints and what constitutes consumer harm.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 18 - Consumer Protection Councils

Establishes councils to promote and protect consumer rights.

Explanation:
These councils guide policy and awareness for consumer protection.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 34 - Jurisdiction of District Commission

District Commission handles cases where value is within prescribed limits.

Explanation:
This helps consumers know where to file complaints.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 35 - Filing of complaint

Consumers can file complaints regarding:
- Defective goods
- Deficient services
- Unfair trade practices

Explanation:
This section provides the formal process to initiate legal action.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 38 - Procedure on admission

Commission examines complaint and may:
- Admit or reject it
- Order investigation

Explanation:
Ensures structured processing of consumer complaints.

--------------------------------------------------

Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005

--------------------------------------------------

Section 2 - Definitions

Defines domestic relationship, aggrieved person, and respondent.

Explanation:
Clarifies who is protected under the Act.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 3 - Domestic violence

Includes:
- Physical abuse
- Emotional abuse
- Economic abuse

Explanation:
Broad definition ensures comprehensive protection.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 12 - Application to Magistrate

Victim can file application seeking protection orders.

Explanation:
Starts legal proceedings for protection.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 17 - Right to residence

Woman has the right to reside in shared household.

Explanation:
Prevents forced eviction.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 18 - Protection orders

Court can restrain abuser from committing violence.

Explanation:
Provides immediate legal protection.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 19 - Residence orders

Court can ensure safe residence for victim.

Explanation:
Secures housing rights.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 20 - Monetary relief

Court may order financial support for victim.

Explanation:
Covers medical expenses, loss of earnings, etc.

--------------------------------------------------

Industrial Disputes Act
Section 2: Definitions.
Section 10: Reference of disputes to Boards, Courts or Tribunals.
Section 11A: Powers of Labour Courts, Tribunals and National Tribunals to give appropriate relief in case of discharge or dismissal of workmen.
Section 25F: Conditions precedent to retrenchment of workmen.
Section 25G: Procedure for retrenchment.
Section 25H: Re-employment of retrenched workmen.

Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860

--------------------------------------------------

Section 302 - Punishment for murder

Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or life imprisonment.

Explanation:
Applies in cases involving intentional killing.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 354 - Assault on woman

Assault or criminal force against a woman with intent to outrage modesty.

Explanation:
Used in cases of harassment or inappropriate physical conduct.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 376 - Rape

Defines and punishes the offence of rape.

Explanation:
Covers serious sexual offences with strict punishment.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 420 - Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property

Punishes cheating involving fraud and deception.

Explanation:
Commonly used in financial fraud and scam cases.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 498A - Cruelty by husband or relatives

Punishes cruelty or harassment of a married woman.

Explanation:
Used in domestic abuse and dowry-related cases.

--------------------------------------------------

Information Technology Act, 2000

--------------------------------------------------

Section 43 - Damage to computer systems

Covers unauthorized access, data theft, or system damage.

Explanation:
Applies to hacking and cyber intrusions.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 66 - Computer-related offences

Enhances penalties for offences under Section 43 when done dishonestly.

Explanation:
Used in cybercrime involving intent to cause harm.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 66C - Identity theft

Punishes misuse of passwords, digital signatures, or identity.

Explanation:
Applies in online fraud and impersonation cases.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 67 - Publishing obscene content

Punishes publishing or transmitting obscene material online.

Explanation:
Used in cases of inappropriate digital content.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 72 - Breach of confidentiality

Punishes unauthorized disclosure of information.

Explanation:
Protects user data and privacy.

--------------------------------------------------

Section 3 - Responsibility for payment of wages

(1) Every employer shall be responsible for the payment of all wages required to be paid under this Act to persons employed by him.

This responsibility applies to different types of employment situations:

(a) In factories, the manager of the factory is responsible for wage payments.

(b) In industrial or other establishments, the person responsible for supervision and control of the establishment is responsible.

(c) In railways (other than factories), the railway administration or a nominated authority is responsible.

(d) In the case of a contractor, a person designated by the contractor who is directly in charge is responsible.

(e) In any other case, a person designated by the employer is responsible for ensuring compliance with the Act.

In all the above cases, the designated or responsible person must ensure proper and timely payment of wages.

(2) If the contractor or designated person fails to make payment of wages, the employer shall remain ultimately responsible for ensuring that wages are paid.

Explanation:
This section ensures that employees always have a legally accountable authority for receiving wages. Even if intermediaries such as contractors are involved, the employer cannot escape responsibility.
